difficulty.task_excerpt: You are tasked with creating an immunostain panel for using flow cytometry to identify ex vivo peripheral

domain_depth.note: Requires research-frontier knowledge of ILC/NK developmental biology, spectral cytometry instrumentation, and commercial antibody availability across multiple vendors.

reasoning_complexity.note: Solver must simultaneously satisfy ~11 graded criteria spanning instrument compatibility, developmental hierarchy logic, transcription factor co-expression, and dump channel design.

conceptual_leaps.note: Two moderate non-obvious jumps: mapping developmental trajectory onto a single panel architecture, and resolving spectral overlap constraints of the Cytek Aurora across UV/V/B/YG/R laser lines.

plan_length.note: Panel design requires 21+ distinct decisions: selecting each marker, assigning peak channels, defining gating hierarchy for progenitors → precursors → mature subsets, plus dump channel and viability dye.

interdependence.note: Channel assignments constrain which fluorochrome-antibody conjugates are available; a poor early channel allocation cascades into unavailable commercial antibodies or unresolvable spectral overlap for downstream markers.

problem:
Context: Spectral cytometry is becoming a popular tool due to its ability to measure a higher number of fluorescently labeled antibodies, providing higher resolution of heterogeneous cell populations. ILCs and NK cells share a developmental trajectory that can be identified based on the expression of surface receptors and transcription factors. Being able to create complex flow panels that allow you to identify and measure expression of proteins of interest in specific populations is key for researchers.
Question: You are tasked with creating an immunostain panel for using flow cytometry to identify ex vivo peripheral blood and tonsil NK cell and ILC developmental subsets to measure lineage-specific transcription factors through differentiation. You have access to an aurora spectral cytometer, cryopreserved samples, biolegend, BD biosciences and miltenyi antibodies, and essential lab equipment.

Think step by step and solve the problem below. In your answer, you should include all intermediate derivations, formulas, important steps, and justifications for how you arrived at your answer. Be as detailed as possible in your response.

hierarchy.global: Design a validated multi-parameter spectral flow cytometry panel for the Aurora instrument that identifies NK cell and ILC developmental subsets from cryopreserved peripheral blood and tonsil by combining surface lineage markers with intracellular transcription factor staining, then specify the complete staining protocol and control strategy.

subtasks.subtask: Define the biological gating hierarchy and target antigen list
atomics: Enumerate lineage dump antigens to exclude non-ILC/NK cells: CD3 (T cells), CD19/CD20 (B cells), CD14 (monocytes), CD66b (granulocytes)

Select ILC/NK lineage-defining surface markers: CD127 (IL-7Rα, marks ILC lineage), CD56 (NK and NNK1 ILCs), CD117 (c-Kit, ILC progenitors/ILC3), CD16 (mature NK), NKp46 (NK identity), CRTH2/CD294 (ILC2), NKp44 (ILC3), CD161 (pan-ILC/NK)
Select developmental and tissue-residency markers: CD34 (HSC/progenitor), CD45RA (differentiation stage), CD94 (NK maturation), CD103 (tissue residency for tonsil subset)
Select intracellular transcription factors: T-bet/TBX21 (ILC1/NK), GATA3 (ILC2), RORγt/RORC (ILC3), Eomes (mature NK cells)

Map hierarchical gating strategy: singlets → live → Lin-dump- → CD56+CD127- (NK) vs CD56-/lowCD127+ (ILCs) → subset gates (ILC1 T-bet+, ILC2 CRTH2+GATA3+, ILC3 NKp44+RORγt+, NK dim/bright by CD56/CD16/Eomes)

subtasks.subtask: Assign fluorochromes to antigens using spectral cytometry panel design principles
atomics: Assign viability dye to UV laser: Zombie UV Fixable Viability Kit (BioLegend) to discriminate dead cells in cryopreserved samples before fixation
Assign lineage dump cocktail to a single bright fluorochrome (e.g., PE or BV785) to allow boolean gating; conjugate CD3/CD19/CD14/CD20/CD66b all to same fluorochrome for simple Lin- gate
Assign brightest fluorochromes (BV421, PE, APC) to dimmest or most critical antigens: T-bet→PE-CF594, GATA3→BV421, RORγt→APC (intracellular TFs need brightness due to fixation signal loss)
Assign mid-brightness fluorochromes to moderately expressed surface antigens: CD127→BV510, CD117→BUV737, CD56→BUV395, CD16→PerCP-Cy5.5, NKp46→AF647
Assign remaining fluorochromes to brightly expressed or less critical antigens: CRTH2→PE-Cy5, NKp44→BV605, CD94→BV650, CD161→FITC, Eomes→PE-Cy7, CD34→BV786, CD103→BUV661

Verify Aurora laser lines (355 nm UV, 405 nm violet, 488 nm blue, 561 nm yellow-green, 638 nm red) accommodate all selected fluorochromes and that spectral signatures are sufficiently distinct for unmixing

subtasks.subtask: Select specific antibody clones and vendors for each target
atomics: Select BioLegend clones: anti-CD3 UCHT1 PE, anti-CD19 HIB19 PE, anti-CD14 M5E2 PE, anti-CD20 2H7 PE, anti-CD66b G10F5 PE (dump); anti-CD127 A019D5 BV510; anti-CRTH2 BM16 PE-Cy5; anti-NKp46 9E2 AF647; anti-CD161 HP-3G10 FITC; anti-GATA3 16E10A23 BV421; Zombie UV viability

Select BD Biosciences clones: anti-CD56 NCAM16.2 BUV395; anti-CD16 3G8 PerCP-Cy5.5; anti-CD117 104D2 BUV737; anti-CD94 HP-3D9 BV650; anti-CD34 8G12 BV786; anti-T-bet O4-46 PE-CF594; anti-Eomes WD1928 PE-Cy7; anti-CD103 Ber-ACT8 BUV661
Select Miltenyi clones: anti-NKp44 2.29 BV605; anti-RORγt REA278 APC
Confirm all clones are validated for intracellular staining where applicable (T-bet, GATA3, RORγt, Eomes) and for use with spectral cytometry

subtasks.subtask: Develop the complete staining protocol for cryopreserved samples
atomics: Thaw cryopreserved PBMCs/tonsil cells rapidly at 37°C, wash in warm RPMI+10% FBS, count viable cells, rest 1–2 h at 37°C to recover surface antigen expression
Perform Fc receptor block (Human TruStain FcX, BioLegend) for 10 min at 4°C to reduce non-specific antibody binding
Incubate cells with surface antibody cocktail (all surface markers plus viability dye) for 30 min at 4°C in PBS/2%FBS, then wash twice
Fix and permeabilize cells using Foxp3/Transcription Factor Staining Buffer Set (eBioscience/Thermo) or BD Cytofix/Cytoperm: fix 45 min at 4°C, wash with permeabilization buffer
Incubate cells with intracellular antibody cocktail (T-bet, GATA3, RORγt, Eomes) in permeabilization buffer for 45 min at 4°C, wash twice, resuspend in PBS/2%FBS for acquisition

subtasks.subtask: Design and prepare controls for spectral unmixing and gating validation
atomics: Prepare single-stain controls for each fluorochrome using antibody-capture beads (UltraComp eBeads for antibodies, ArC beads for viability dye) to generate spectral reference libraries for Aurora SpectroFlo unmixing
Prepare Fluorescence Minus One (FMO) controls for all markers used in subset gates (CD127, CD117, CD56, CRTH2, NKp44, T-bet, GATA3, RORγt, Eomes, CD16) to set accurate positive/negative thresholds
Include unstained and isotype-matched control samples to assess non-specific binding background
Include known positive controls: tonsil samples expected to be enriched in ILC3 (NKp44+RORγt+), and peripheral blood expected to be enriched in mature NK cells (CD56dimCD16+Eomes+)

global_atomics: Titrate each antibody on pilot samples to determine optimal concentration that maximizes staining index while minimizing background, particularly for intracellular targets after fixation

Run the full panel on Aurora SpectroFlo software, check unmixing quality metrics (root mean square error per channel), and verify that rare populations (ILC progenitors <0.1% of Lin- cells) are identifiable above background

Validate the panel by confirming expected biological patterns: ILC2 enrichment in tonsil vs. blood, CD56bright NK cells as ~10% of NK pool in blood, RORγt/GATA3/T-bet mutual exclusivity defining ILC1/2/3 subsets

gold_actions.global: Construct a 20-parameter Aurora spectral cytometry panel combining a UV viability dye, PE-conjugated lineage dump (CD3/CD19/CD14/CD20/CD66b), 10 surface markers (CD56-BUV395, CD127-BV510, CD117-BUV737, CD16-PerCP-Cy5.5, NKp46-AF647, CRTH2-PE-Cy5, NKp44-BV605, CD94-BV650, CD161-FITC, CD34-BV786, CD103-BUV661), and 4 intracellular transcription factors (T-bet-PE-CF594, GATA3-BV421, RORγt-APC, Eomes-PE-Cy7), using eBioscience Foxp3/TF fixation buffer with single-stain bead controls and FMO gating controls

subtasks.subtask: Define the biological gating hierarchy and target antigen list for NK/ILC developmental subsets
atomics: Enumerate dump channel targets as CD3, CD19, CD20, CD14, CD66b all conjugated to PE to create a single Lin-PE- gate excluding T cells, B cells, monocytes, and granulocytes

Select surface ILC/NK markers: CD127 (ILC lineage), CD56 (NK/ILC1), CD117 (ILC progenitors/ILC3), CD16 (mature NK), NKp46 (NK-specific), CRTH2 (ILC2), NKp44 (ILC3), CD161 (pan-ILC/NK), CD94 (NK maturation), CD34 (progenitor), CD103 (tissue residency)
Select intracellular transcription factors T-bet (ILC1/NK1), GATA3 (ILC2), RORγt (ILC3), Eomes (mature NK) as the developmental readout layer

Map sequential gating: FSC-A/SSC-A (lymphocytes) → singlets (FSC-H/FSC-A) → live (Zombie UV-) → Lin-PE- → CD56+CD127- (NK pool: dim=mature, bright=immature) vs. CD56-/lowCD127+ (ILC pool: CRTH2+ ILC2, NKp44+ ILC3, T-bet+CRTH2-NKp44- ILC1) → transcription factor expression within each gate

subtasks.subtask: Assign fluorochromes to all antigens optimized for Aurora spectral unmixing
atomics: Assign Zombie UV (BioLegend, 405 nm excitation, ~450 nm emission) as the viability discriminator compatible with downstream fixation steps
Assign all 5 dump antigens (CD3 UCHT1, CD19 HIB19, CD14 M5E2, CD20 2H7, CD66b G10F5) to PE (BioLegend) to create a single-channel boolean dump gate

Assign brightest fluorochromes to intracellular TFs: GATA3→BV421 (high brightness, violet laser, BioLegend clone 16E10A23), T-bet→PE-CF594 (BD clone O4-46, yellow-green excitable, high SI), RORγt→APC (Miltenyi clone REA278, red laser, high brightness), Eomes→PE-Cy7 (BD clone WD1928, high brightness for rare NK subsets)

Assign mid-bright fluorochromes to key surface discriminators: CD56→BUV395 (BD, UV laser, separates bright/dim NK), CD127→BV510 (BioLegend, violet, good signal for ILCs), CD117→BUV737 (BD, UV, progenitor identification), CD16→PerCP-Cy5.5 (BioLegend/BD, blue laser, NK maturation)

Assign remaining fluorochromes to bright or secondary markers: NKp46→AF647 (BioLegend clone 9E2, red laser), CRTH2→PE-Cy5 (BioLegend clone BM16), NKp44→BV605 (Miltenyi clone 2.29), CD94→BV650 (BD clone HP-3D9), CD161→FITC (BioLegend clone HP-3G10), CD34→BV786 (BD clone 8G12), CD103→BUV661 (BD clone Ber-ACT8)

Verify spectral signatures on Aurora SpectroFlo software that all 20 fluorochromes (including UV viability) generate distinguishable reference spectra across all 64 detector channels on the 5-laser Aurora configuration

subtasks.subtask: Select validated antibody clones from BioLegend, BD Biosciences, and Miltenyi for each target
atomics: Order from BioLegend: CD3 UCHT1-PE, CD19 HIB19-PE, CD14 M5E2-PE, CD20 2H7-PE, CD66b G10F5-PE (dump cocktail), CD127 A019D5-BV510, CRTH2 BM16-PE-Cy5, NKp46 9E2-AF647, CD161 HP-3G10-FITC, GATA3 16E10A23-BV421, Zombie UV fixable viability kit
Order from BD Biosciences: CD56 NCAM16.2-BUV395, CD16 3G8-PerCP-Cy5.5, CD117 104D2-BUV737, CD94 HP-3D9-BV650, CD34 8G12-BV786, T-bet O4-46-PE-CF594, Eomes WD1928-PE-Cy7, CD103 Ber-ACT8-BUV661
Order from Miltenyi: NKp44 2.29-BV605, RORγt REA278-APC (REA clones recommended for spectral cytometry due to minimal background)

Verify each clone's validation data for human peripheral blood and tonsil tissue, and for intracellular application post-fixation where applicable (T-bet O4-46, GATA3 16E10A23, RORγt REA278, Eomes WD1928)

subtasks.subtask: Execute the complete staining protocol for cryopreserved PBMC and tonsil samples
atomics: Thaw vials at 37°C for 60 s, transfer dropwise to 9 mL warm RPMI+10% FBS+DNase I (50 U/mL), centrifuge 400×g 5 min, count live cells with trypan blue, rest at 37°C/5% CO2 for 1–2 h to recover internalized surface markers
Block Fc receptors with 5 μL Human TruStain FcX (BioLegend) per 10^6 cells in 100 μL PBS/2% FBS for 10 min on ice

Add surface antibody master mix (lineage dump cocktail + CD56, CD127, CD117, CD16, NKp46, CRTH2, NKp44, CD94, CD161, CD34, CD103 + Zombie UV) to blocked cells, incubate 30 min at 4°C protected from light, wash twice with PBS/2% FBS
Fix cells with 1× eBioscience Foxp3 Fixation/Permeabilization buffer (250 μL per sample) for 45 min at 4°C, wash twice with 1× Permeabilization Buffer, count fixed cells

Resuspend fixed/permeabilized cells in 100 μL Permeabilization Buffer, add intracellular antibody mix (T-bet-PE-CF594, GATA3-BV421, RORγt-APC, Eomes-PE-Cy7) at pre-titrated concentrations, incubate 45 min at 4°C, wash twice with Permeabilization Buffer, resuspend in PBS/2% FBS for same-day Aurora acquisition

subtasks.subtask: Prepare single-stain bead controls and FMO controls for Aurora unmixing and gate setting
atomics: Prepare 20 single-stain tubes: one UltraComp eBead tube per fluorochrome-conjugated antibody (add titrated antibody to beads per manufacturer protocol) and one ArC reactive bead tube for Zombie UV viability dye; process bead controls through the same fixation/permeabilization steps as cells

Prepare FMO control tubes for each gating marker (CD127, CD117, CD56, CD16, CRTH2, NKp44, NKp46, T-bet, GATA3, RORγt, Eomes, CD94): full-panel stained cells omitting one marker per tube, to define the fluorescence spread into the gate of interest

global_atomics: Perform antibody titration experiment on pilot blood and tonsil samples: test 4-point dilution series (1:25, 1:50, 1:100, 1:200) for each antibody, calculate staining index (SI = (MFI_pos - MFI_neg) / (2 × SD_neg)) for each dilution, select concentration maximizing SI; repeat for intracellular antibodies post-fixation where SI typically drops 2–3-fold versus surface staining

Acquire all samples and controls on the Aurora spectral cytometer using SpectroFlo software: perform spectral unmixing using the 20-color reference library from single-stain bead controls, check per-channel root-mean-square error (RMSE <0.02 AU indicates good unmixing), flag any populations with autofluorescence artifacts using the autofluorescence reference spectrum extracted from unstained cells

Validate panel biological outputs: confirm CD56bright NK (CD56hiCD16-Eomes-/lowT-bet+) represents ~10% of NK pool in blood and is enriched in tonsil; confirm ILC2 (Lin-CD127+CRTH2+GATA3+) enrichment in tonsil vs. peripheral blood; confirm mutual exclusivity of GATA3, RORγt, and T-bet expression across ILC subsets as a cross-validation of panel specificity and unmixing fidelity

difficulty.task_excerpt: In devising a drug delivery system for an in vitro experiment using cultured hepatic cells, a PhD student

domain_depth.note: Requires graduate-level knowledge spanning DNA nanotechnology, nanobody engineering, orphan receptor biology, and intracellular membrane trafficking.

reasoning_complexity.note: Solver must chain: nanobody binds orphan receptor → receptor lacks canonical endocytic signal → payload still enters cell → therefore constitutive membrane protein recycling must be the mechanism.

conceptual_leaps.note: Three non-trivial jumps required: (1) recognize that surface proteins are constitutively internalized independent of signaling; (2) connect this to endosomal recycling pathways; (3) distinguish constitutive recycling from canonical receptor-mediated endocytosis.

plan_length.note: Solution involves roughly 3–5 reasoning steps; no lengthy computation, code, or multi-turn workflow.

interdependence.note: Each inferential step strictly depends on the previous one; misidentifying the mechanism at any stage derails the final answer.

problem:
In devising a drug delivery system for an in vitro experiment using cultured hepatic cells, a PhD student designed three different approaches:

- Covalently linking the payload (hereby a small non-bioactive hydrophobic molecule) to a DNA origami structure
- Conjugating the payload to a nanobody designed to target a currently unidentified surface orphan receptor.
- Linking the payload to a DNA tetrahedron with 20nt-long sides

Due to the difficulty of synthesizing DNA nanostructures, the researcher decided to focus on the nanobody approach. After trying to identify the target of the orphan receptors to figure out if it is involved in a phagocytosis-like process, the student's PI told them that the identification does not matter and it would work even if this receptor does not canonically induce endocytosis. What cellular process is the PI depending upon for this novel approach to work?

Think step by step and solve the problem below. At the end of your response, write your final answer on a new line starting with “FINAL ANSWER”. It should be an answer to the question such as providing a number, mathematical expression, formula, or entity name, without any extra commentary or providing multiple answer attempts.

hierarchy.global: Identify the cellular process the PI depends on for a nanobody-conjugated hydrophobic payload to enter hepatic cells via an orphan receptor that does not canonically induce endocytosis.

subtasks.subtask: Characterize the payload and its physicochemical properties
atomics: Note that the payload is described as a small, non-bioactive, hydrophobic molecule
Recall that hydrophobic molecules have high membrane permeability due to affinity for lipid bilayers
Recognize that hydrophobicity enables passive diffusion across phospholipid membranes without active transport

subtasks.subtask: Analyze the nanobody delivery mechanism and what receptor binding accomplishes
atomics: Establish that the nanobody binds a surface orphan receptor on hepatic cells, concentrating the payload at the outer leaflet of the plasma membrane
Recognize that receptor binding localizes the hydrophobic payload in immediate proximity to the lipid bilayer
Identify that membrane proximity allows partitioning of the hydrophobic molecule into the bilayer independent of receptor signaling

subtasks.subtask: Evaluate why endocytosis is not required and what alternative uptake route exists
atomics: Confirm the PI's statement implies the drug enters cells even without receptor-mediated endocytosis
Rule out clathrin-mediated endocytosis, macropinocytosis, and phagocytosis as requirements since receptor does not canonically induce them
Conclude that passive transcellular diffusion through the lipid bilayer, driven by the hydrophobic nature of the payload, is the operative mechanism

subtasks.subtask: Reconcile the nanobody-receptor interaction with passive diffusion as the entry route
atomics: Understand that the nanobody serves only as a targeting/localization moiety to bring the payload to the cell surface, not to trigger endocytosis
Confirm that once the hydrophobic payload is membrane-proximal, it spontaneously partitions into and traverses the bilayer via passive diffusion

global_atomics: Verify that passive diffusion is consistent with all constraints: hydrophobic payload, no required endocytosis, orphan receptor, hepatic cell context
State the answer: the PI depends on passive (membrane) diffusion for intracellular drug delivery

gold_actions.global: Determine that passive diffusion through the lipid bilayer is the cellular process enabling hydrophobic payload uptake when receptor-mediated endocytosis is absent.

subtasks.subtask: Identify payload as small hydrophobic molecule enabling membrane permeation
atomics: Classify payload as hydrophobic → high partition coefficient into lipid bilayer
Apply Overton's rule: lipid solubility predicts passive membrane permeability
Conclude hydrophobic molecules cross membranes by passive diffusion without protein assistance

subtasks.subtask: Determine that nanobody-receptor binding concentrates payload at the membrane surface
atomics: Map nanobody → orphan receptor binding event to surface localization of hydrophobic payload
Recognize membrane proximity creates favorable thermodynamic gradient for hydrophobic partitioning into bilayer
Establish that receptor signaling is not required for this partitioning step

subtasks.subtask: Exclude endocytosis and identify passive diffusion as the actual uptake mechanism
atomics: Eliminate receptor-mediated endocytosis (orphan receptor lacks canonical endocytic signaling)
Eliminate phagocytosis (payload is a small molecule, not a particle; process not relevant here)
Identify passive transcellular diffusion as the mechanism consistent with all stated constraints

subtasks.subtask: Confirm nanobody role is targeting not internalization signaling
atomics: Assign nanobody function = cell-surface targeting/anchoring only
Confirm hydrophobic molecule dissociates or diffuses from conjugate into bilayer upon membrane contact

global_atomics: Cross-check: hydrophobic + membrane-proximal + no endocytosis required → passive diffusion is the only consistent answer
Final answer: passive (membrane) diffusion

difficulty.task_excerpt: The protein TDP-43, implicated in ALS and FTD, is known to partition into stress granules (SGs).
difficulty.domain: biology (molecular neuroscience, protein biochemistry, phase separation)

domain_depth.note: Requires research-frontier knowledge spanning LLPS biology, TDP-43 pathomechanisms, RRM domain redox chemistry, and lipid-protein interactions in condensates.

reasoning_complexity.note: Must integrate multiple molecular mechanisms (intra-condensate demixing, disulfide bonding, α-helix to β-sheet transition, fatty acid effects on condensate properties) into a coherent predictive framework.

conceptual_leaps.note: Requires non-obvious insights: that oxidative stress causes RRM1 local unfolding to expose cysteines, that demixing precedes aggregation, and that fatty acids alter condensate microenvironment to modulate these dynamics — none of which are derivable from first principles alone.

plan_length.note: Roughly 6–8 distinct reasoning steps: describe SG recruitment, identify intra-condensate demixing, link oxidative stress to RRM1 unfolding, link cysteine exposure to disulfide bonding, analyze stabilized RRM1 variant, analyze fatty acid elevation, and synthesize combined effect.

interdependence.note: Each mechanistic step strictly depends on the prior: missing the demixing step or the cysteine-exposure mechanism entirely undermines the prediction about the RRM1 variant and the combined effect.

problem:
Context: The misfolding and aggregation of specific proteins are central to the pathology of many neurodegenerative diseases. While these proteins are often soluble and functional under normal physiological conditions, cellular stress can trigger their accumulation into aberrant assemblies. Biomolecular condensates, formed through liquid-liquid phase separation (LLPS), are increasingly recognized as important cellular compartments, but their precise role in the initiation of pathological protein aggregation remains an area of intense investigation. Understanding the specific microenvironment within these condensates and the molecular events that tip the balance from dynamic, functional assemblies to irreversible, pathological aggregates is critical for developing therapeutic strategies.
Question: The protein TDP-43, implicated in ALS and FTD, is known to partition into stress granules (SGs).

1. Describe the proposed molecular pathway by which TDP-43 transitions to a pathological aggregate within SGs, highlighting the significance of intra-condensate demixing. 
2. Consider a motor neuron experiencing chronic oxidative stress (inducing SGs with TDP-43 recruitment). This neuron simultaneously exhibits two specific molecular alterations:
   - A genetic variant conferring significantly increased structural stability to TDP-43's RRM1 domain. 
   - A sustained five-fold elevation in the intracellular concentration of short-chain (C6-C10) saturated fatty acids. 

Analyze and predict the combined consequence of these alterations on TDP-43's aggregation propensity within SGs, compared to the wild-type scenario. Provide a mechanistic justification for your prediction.

Think step by step and solve the problem below. In your answer, you should include all intermediate derivations, formulas, important steps, and justifications for how you arrived at your answer. Be as detailed as possible in your response.

hierarchy.global: Reconstruct the intra-condensate demixing pathway by which SG-resident TDP-43 converts to pathological aggregates, then mechanistically predict how hyperstabilized RRM1 and elevated C6-C10 saturated fatty acids synergistically shift TDP-43 aggregation propensity relative to wild-type.

subtasks.subtask: Establish TDP-43 domain architecture and normal partitioning into stress granules under physiological stress
atomics: Identify TDP-43's functional domains: N-terminal domain, RRM1, RRM2 (RNA binding), and C-terminal low-complexity/prion-like domain (LCD) driving LLPS
Describe how cytoplasmic stress triggers SG nucleation via eIF2α phosphorylation and stalled translation initiation complexes
Explain how TDP-43's LCD mediates weak multivalent interactions that drive its partitioning into SG condensate via LLPS

subtasks.subtask: Describe the molecular pathway of intra-condensate demixing and conversion of dynamic TDP-43 liquid droplets to irreversible pathological aggregates
atomics: Define intra-condensate demixing: secondary phase separation within an existing SG producing a TDP-43-enriched dense sub-phase with locally elevated protein concentration
Explain how the high local TDP-43 concentration in the demixed sub-phase exceeds the nucleation threshold, promoting LCD-driven intermolecular beta-sheet stacking and amyloid-like fibril nucleation

Identify post-translational modifications (hyperphosphorylation at S409/S410, ubiquitination, caspase-3 cleavage yielding 25 kDa C-terminal fragments) that accumulate in the dense phase and lock aggregates irreversibly

Describe the material state transition: liquid SG → gel-like demixed TDP-43 sub-phase → solid amyloid core, emphasizing that dissolution failure after stress resolution leaves aggregates that seed further misfolding

subtasks.subtask: Analyze the mechanistic consequences of hyperstabilized RRM1 on TDP-43 dynamics and aggregation within SGs
atomics: Note that RRM1 normally binds UG-rich RNA motifs, and this RNA occupancy sterically shields the LCD from aberrant intermolecular contacts and slows condensate aging

Reason that a rigidly hyperstabilized RRM1 reduces conformational adaptability required for productive RNA engagement, leading to diminished RNA-loading and faster RNA release kinetics inside the condensate
Predict that loss of RNA-buffering unmasks the LCD, lowers the effective concentration threshold for intra-condensate demixing, and accelerates nucleation of amyloid-competent TDP-43 assemblies
Note a secondary effect: hyperstable RRM1 may resist chaperone-assisted unfolding/refolding cycles that normally restore solubility, compounding aggregate accumulation

subtasks.subtask: Analyze how a sustained five-fold elevation of C6-C10 saturated fatty acids alters condensate biophysics and TDP-43 aggregation propensity
atomics: Establish that short-chain saturated fatty acids (C6-C10: caproic, caprylic, capric) can partition into hydrophobic microdomains within condensates and interact with exposed hydrophobic patches on TDP-43's LCD

Explain that saturated fatty acids, lacking double-bond kinks, pack tightly and increase local viscosity/rigidity within the condensate interior, shifting the condensate material state toward gel or solid
Predict that elevated C6-C10 fatty acids reduce the energy barrier for the liquid-to-solid transition within SGs, making irreversible gelation kinetically favored

Note that under chronic oxidative stress, short-chain saturated fatty acids can undergo limited peroxidation to generate reactive aldehydic species (e.g., malondialdehyde), which form covalent adducts on TDP-43 lysine/histidine residues, introducing crosslinks that stabilize aggregates against dissolution

subtasks.subtask: Integrate both alterations and predict combined effect on TDP-43 aggregation propensity relative to wild-type, with mechanistic justification
atomics: Identify that the two alterations act on orthogonal mechanisms: hyperstable RRM1 increases effective TDP-43 free-LCD concentration (removing RNA-mediated buffering), while elevated saturated fatty acids lower the kinetic and thermodynamic barrier for the liquid-to-solid transition

Predict a synergistic ('double-hit') amplification: the RNA-unshielded LCD reaches demixing threshold faster (alteration 1) into a condensate environment already primed for gelation (alteration 2), dramatically accelerating conversion to irreversible aggregates compared to either perturbation alone

Compare to wild-type: WT TDP-43 is RNA-bound (LCD shielded), SG condensates remain liquid, demixing is slow and reversible upon stress resolution; the double mutant scenario produces rapid, irreversible aggregation even under moderate chronic stress

Conclude that the combined consequence is a strongly elevated aggregation propensity: both the nucleation rate (faster due to unshielded LCD) and the probability of irreversible solidification (higher due to fatty-acid-induced condensate rigidification) are increased, predicting earlier onset and greater burden of cytoplasmic TDP-43 inclusions characteristic of ALS/FTD pathology

global_atomics: Verify internal consistency: confirm that each proposed mechanism (RNA-shielding loss, condensate gelation, PTM accumulation) references experimentally established TDP-43 or LLPS biology and is not contradicted by the problem constraints

Sanity-check directionality: confirm all predicted effects converge on increased rather than decreased aggregation, and that no proposed mechanism (e.g., hyperstable RRM1 blocking fibril template exposure) would partially oppose the conclusion

gold_actions.global: Map TDP-43 SG-to-aggregate conversion via intra-condensate demixing, then apply mechanistic reasoning to predict that hyperstable RRM1 (removes RNA-mediated LCD shielding) combined with elevated C6-C10 saturated fatty acids (rigidifies condensate, lowers liquid-to-solid transition barrier) synergistically and dramatically increases TDP-43 pathological aggregation propensity beyond either perturbation alone.

subtasks.subtask: Establish TDP-43 domain architecture and SG partitioning
atomics: Assign functional roles: RRM1+RRM2 bind UG-rich RNA and maintain nuclear/cytoplasmic shuttling; C-terminal LCD (residues ~277-414, glycine-rich prion-like domain) drives LLPS via weak pi-pi, cation-pi, and hydrophobic contacts

Identify stress-induced SG assembly: eIF2α phosphorylation by HRI/PKR/GCN2/PERK stalls translation, freeing mRNPs that nucleate SGs via LLPS of G3BP1/2 scaffold proteins, into which TDP-43 is recruited via its LCD
Specify that TDP-43 partitions into SGs in a concentration- and interaction-dependent manner, remaining dynamic (fast exchange, liquid-like) under acute, resolvable stress

subtasks.subtask: Reconstruct intra-condensate demixing pathway to irreversible TDP-43 aggregates
atomics: Define intra-condensate demixing as a secondary spinodal decomposition event within the SG, producing a TDP-43-dense sub-phase (local [TDP-43] >> bulk SG concentration) that is thermodynamically metastable

Specify that within this dense sub-phase, TDP-43 LCD undergoes nucleated polymerization: beta-strand-forming segments (e.g., residues 312-317, 370-374) stack intermolecularly, transitioning from disordered monomers to oligomeric nuclei to amyloid-like fibrils

List pathology-associated PTMs that accumulate in the demixed phase: phospho-S409/S410 (detected by pTDP-43 antibody in ALS inclusions), ubiquitin conjugation, and caspase-3 cleavage generating aggregation-prone 25 kDa C-terminal fragments enriched in the LCD

Describe material state sequence: SG liquid droplet (τ_exchange ~ seconds) → TDP-43-enriched gel sub-phase (τ_exchange ~ minutes-hours) → amyloid solid core (irreversible, τ_dissolution → ∞), where failure to dissolve after stress resolution seeds further templated aggregation

subtasks.subtask: Mechanistically analyze hyperstable RRM1 effect on TDP-43 SG behavior
atomics: Establish baseline: WT RRM1 binds UG-rich RNA (Kd ~10-100 nM), and RNA occupancy physically occludes LCD from forming intermolecular contacts, effectively buffering demixing; RNA also promotes productive condensate exchange

Apply the stability variant: rigidly hyperstabilized RRM1 (e.g., increased ΔG_fold by 3-5 kcal/mol via buried hydrophobic substitution or disulfide engineering) has reduced conformational flexibility in beta1-alpha1-beta2 loops critical for RNA kinking accommodation, resulting in diminished RNA-binding affinity or occupancy time within the condensate

Derive consequence: reduced RNA loading → LCD is unshielded → effective free LCD concentration available for intermolecular interaction increases → intra-condensate demixing nucleation threshold is reached at lower total TDP-43 concentration and shorter timescale

Add secondary effect: hyperstable RRM1 resists Hsp70/Hsp40 chaperone-mediated conformational remodeling that normally extracts misfolded TDP-43 from nascent aggregates, reducing aggregate clearance rate

subtasks.subtask: Mechanistically analyze elevated C6-C10 saturated fatty acids on condensate properties and TDP-43 aggregation
atomics: Establish partitioning: amphiphilic C6-C10 saturated fatty acids (pKa ~4.8, largely protonated at cytoplasmic pH ~7.2 under stress) insert their acyl chains into hydrophobic pockets within the TDP-43 LCD and into the hydrophobic core of the condensate interior

Apply packing argument: saturated acyl chains (no double bonds, fully extended all-trans conformation) maximize van der Waals contacts and increase condensate interior viscosity (η) and elastic modulus (G'), shifting the condensate from Maxwell-fluid liquid (low G', fast stress relaxation) toward a viscoelastic gel

Predict kinetic consequence: elevated η reduces the diffusion coefficient of TDP-43 within the condensate (D = kT/6πηr), slowing dynamic exchange with the bulk, trapping TDP-43 in the demixed sub-phase longer, and increasing residence time available for fibril nucleation—a positive feedback on aggregation

Identify chemical modification route under chronic oxidative stress: even short-chain saturated fatty acids undergo limited lipid peroxidation generating malondialdehyde and 4-hydroxynonenal (4-HNE)-like aldehydes at five-fold elevation, which form Schiff base and Michael adducts on TDP-43 Lys84, Lys145, His168, crosslinking monomers and locking aggregates against chaperone-mediated disaggregation

subtasks.subtask: Synthesize combined prediction with quantitative reasoning and WT comparison
atomics: Identify orthogonal mechanisms: hyperstable RRM1 acts on the nucleation side (increases available free LCD → lowers critical nucleation concentration Cn*), while elevated saturated fatty acids act on the propagation/irreversibility side (increases condensate viscosity → slows exchange, promotes gelation; generates crosslinking adducts → prevents dissolution)

Predict synergistic outcome: nucleation rate J ∝ exp(-ΔG*_nucl/kT) increases because Cn* is lowered (more free LCD available); simultaneously the rate of fibril elongation relative to dissolution increases because reduced exchange kinetics favor monomer addition over escape; the product of these effects is a multiplicatively elevated aggregation flux compared to either perturbation alone

Contrast with WT: in WT motor neuron, RRM1 is RNA-loaded (LCD shielded, Cn* high), condensate is liquid (fast exchange, aggregation reversible), no excess fatty acid crosslinking; combined alterations remove all three protective factors simultaneously, predicting earlier onset, higher steady-state aggregate burden, and reduced response to stress resolution

State final mechanistic prediction: the motor neuron with hyperstable RRM1 + elevated C6-C10 saturated fatty acids will exhibit markedly accelerated formation of cytoplasmic phospho-TDP-43-positive, ubiquitin-positive inclusions consistent with ALS/FTD pathology, driven by (1) RNA-shielding loss accelerating demixing nucleation and (2) condensate gelation plus covalent crosslinking rendering aggregates irreversible

global_atomics: Cross-check that hyperstable RRM1 does not paradoxically inhibit aggregation by blocking LCD exposure through a clamp mechanism—confirm this is ruled out because hyperstability reduces RNA engagement (the primary LCD shield) rather than increasing it, and RRM1 itself does not directly mask LCD in the unbound state

Verify that C6-C10 chain length is appropriate for condensate partitioning—confirm that C6-C10 is sufficiently amphiphilic to enter condensate hydrophobic microdomains but short enough to remain cytoplasmic rather than sequestered into lipid droplets, supporting the proposed mechanism

difficulty.task_excerpt: Biopsies of solid tumors from placebo patients and patients that have been given drug X were collected

domain_depth.note: Requires graduate-level familiarity with scRNA-seq pipelines, PCA interpretation in genomics, batch effects, and dimensionality reduction methods.

reasoning_complexity.note: Solver must cross-reference PCA clustering patterns with experimental metadata to diagnose batch effects, then separately recall the canonical cell-type visualization tool.

conceptual_leaps.note: Two non-obvious jumps: (1) recognizing that patient-wise co-clustering across treatment arms signals batch rather than drug effects, and (2) knowing UMAP specifically is the standard for qualitative cell-population visualization.

plan_length.note: Essentially a two-part short-answer question requiring ~4 distinct reasoning steps: parse PCA groupings, map to batch-effect concept, pivot to second question, recall UMAP.

interdependence.note: The two sub-answers (batch effects; UMAP) are largely independent—failing to identify batch effects does not block recalling UMAP as a visualization method.

problem:
Biopsies of solid tumors from placebo patients and patients that have been given drug X were collected. Total RNA was reverse-transcribed and scRNA-seq was performed. A high-dimensional data matrix (6 patients x 100,000 cells x 9,000 genes) is collected and principal component analysis (PCA) was performed. Plotting a scatter plot of treated (T) and untreated samples (C) (6 patients each, numbered 1 to 6) with the axes being PC1 and PC2, there are clear separations between (C1, C3), (T1, T3), (T4, T5, C6), (T2, C5, C6), and (T6, C2, C4). These results suggest the presence of .... effects. The researcher has some idea that the cell types in his samples are very diverse. What is the method that the researcher can apply to visualize and qualitatively assess the cell types/populations are within the samples?

Think step by step and solve the problem below. At the end of your response, write your final answer on a new line starting with “FINAL ANSWER”. It should be an answer to the question such as providing a number, mathematical expression, formula, or entity name, without any extra commentary or providing multiple answer attempts.

hierarchy.global: Interpret the scRNA-seq PCA clustering pattern to identify the confounding biological/technical effect driving sample separation, then identify the standard nonlinear dimensionality-reduction method used to visualize diverse cell-type populations in single-cell data.

subtasks.subtask: Decode the PCA scatter plot cluster membership to determine what drives sample separation
atomics: Enumerate the five PCA clusters: {C1,C3}, {T1,T3}, {T4,T5,C6}, {T2,C5,C6}, {T6,C2,C4}
Check whether each cluster is composed of purely treated (T) or purely control (C) samples, or a mixture of both
Determine that clusters do not segregate by treatment label but instead mix T and C samples from different patient IDs

subtasks.subtask: Name the biological or technical confound revealed by the mixed T/C clustering pattern
atomics: Recognize that co-clustering of T and C samples across different patient numbers is the hallmark of patient-specific (inter-individual) variability dominating over treatment signal
Label the phenomenon as batch effects (patient effects / inter-individual biological variability) rather than a drug response signal

subtasks.subtask: Identify the appropriate visualization method for qualitative assessment of diverse cell-type populations in a 100,000-cell scRNA-seq dataset
atomics: Establish that PCA is a linear method and cannot resolve the non-linear, high-dimensional cell-type structure in 100,000 cells
Recall that UMAP (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection) and t-SNE are the standard nonlinear dimensionality-reduction methods used for scRNA-seq cell-population visualization
Confirm UMAP as the current best-practice method in scRNA-seq pipelines (Seurat, Scanpy) for qualitative cell-type cluster visualization

global_atomics: Verify the two-part answer is complete: (1) batch/patient effects identified from PCA cluster pattern, (2) UMAP named as the cell-type visualization method

gold_actions.global: Map the PCA cluster memberships to diagnose batch/patient effects as the dominant source of variance, then recommend UMAP as the nonlinear visualization method for identifying diverse cell-type populations in scRNA-seq data.

subtasks.subtask: Map five PCA clusters and determine whether treatment or patient identity explains groupings
atomics: Tabulate clusters: {C1,C3} — both controls; {T1,T3} — both treated same patient numbers; {T4,T5,C6}, {T2,C5,C6}, {T6,C2,C4} — each mixes treated and control from different patients
Observe that within most clusters T and C labels co-occur, so treatment status alone does not explain PC1/PC2 separation
Conclude that inter-patient (batch/biological) variation rather than drug effect dominates the top two principal components

subtasks.subtask: Assign correct terminology to the confounding source observed in PCA
atomics: Identify the mixed T/C co-clustering across heterogeneous patient IDs as the signature of batch effects (patient-specific effects)
State that the PCA results suggest the presence of batch effects (inter-individual / patient effects)

subtasks.subtask: Select and justify the visualization method for scRNA-seq cell-type discovery in 100,000-cell data
atomics: Note that PCA's linear projection compresses 9,000-gene space into global variance axes and is insufficient to resolve fine-grained cell-type clusters among 100,000 cells
Specify UMAP (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection) as the nonlinear method that preserves local neighborhood structure and reveals cell-type clusters in 2D
Confirm that UMAP is the standard qualitative cell-population visualization tool in current scRNA-seq analysis workflows (Seurat v4/5, Scanpy/AnnData)

global_atomics: Cross-check both answers against the question: batch effects explains the PCA grouping pattern; UMAP is the method for visualizing and qualitatively assessing cell-type populations

difficulty.task_excerpt: Voting estimator will fail at fit if weights are passed and an estimator is None

domain_depth.note: Requires basic familiarity with scikit-learn's VotingClassifier and how None estimators are handled, but no advanced ML knowledge.

reasoning_complexity.note: The traceback and reproduction script point directly to the offending code path; minimal debugging required.

conceptual_leaps.note: No non-obvious leap — the fix is a straightforward null-check guard before the existing attribute check.

plan_length.note: Fix requires locating one loop in voting.py and inserting two lines (if step is None: continue).

interdependence.note: Single isolated change; no downstream steps depend on each other.

problem:
Voting estimator will fail at fit if weights are passed and an estimator is None
Because we don't check for an estimator to be `None` in `sample_weight` support, `fit` is failing`.

```python
    X, y = load_iris(return_X_y=True)
    voter = VotingClassifier(
        estimators=[('lr', LogisticRegression()),
                    ('rf', RandomForestClassifier())]
    )
    voter.fit(X, y, sample_weight=np.ones(y.shape))
    voter.set_params(lr=None)
    voter.fit(X, y, sample_weight=np.ones(y.shape))
```

```
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'fit'
```

hierarchy.global: Fix VotingClassifier.fit to skip None estimators when checking sample_weight support so that setting an estimator to None via set_params does not raise AttributeError.

difficulty.task_excerpt: A hypothetical organism's cell has n sets of chromosomes. In the F₀ generation of one population of this

domain_depth.note: Requires graduate-level genetics: polyploid organisms, gamete allele composition in n-ploid systems, and multi-generational crossing schemes.

reasoning_complexity.note: Demands constructing a recurrence relation for the maximum number of 'a' alleles achievable each generation, recognizing it as Fibonacci-like, and applying a min-condition to find n.

conceptual_leaps.note: Three non-obvious insights are needed: (1) reframe the problem as tracking the maximum possible 'a' allele count per generation, (2) observe that the crossing scheme (Fₖ = Fₖ₋₁ × Fₖ₋₂) induces a Fibonacci recurrence m(k) = m(k−1) + m(k−2), and (3) derive that 'aaa…' first appears in generation k iff m(k−1) < n ≤ m(k).

plan_length.note: Requires roughly 7–9 distinct steps: set up notation, model gamete contribution for n-ploid, write the recurrence, compute m(0)–m(8), identify the Fibonacci sequence, state the first-appearance condition, and solve for minimum n.

interdependence.note: Each generation's maximum 'a' count depends on the two preceding values; an error in any intermediate m(k) propagates directly into the final answer.

problem:
A hypothetical organism's cell has n sets of chromosomes. In the F₀ generation of one population of this organism, all individuals have genotype aAAAA... (only one 'a' allele). The organism self-crosses in the F₀ generation, and in all subsequent generations, it crosses with the immediate previous generation. If the minimum generation in which a genotype with no 'A' allele (i.e. aaa...) can appear is 8, what is the minimum value of n?

Think step by step and solve the problem below. At the end of your response, write your final answer on a new line starting with “FINAL ANSWER”. It should be an answer to the question such as providing a number, mathematical expression, formula, or entity name, without any extra commentary or providing multiple answer attempts.

PROBLEM:
[Bug]: Confusing deprecation warning when empty data passed to axis with category unit

domain_depth.note: Requires familiarity with Matplotlib internals — unit converters, category handling, and the deprecation warning system.

reasoning_complexity.note: Solver must trace the call chain from ax.plot([],[]) through unit conversion into category.py to identify where the empty-array guard is missing.

conceptual_leaps.note: One key insight: the deprecation warning fires before checking whether the array is empty, so a `values.size` guard must precede the `is_numlike` branch.

plan_length.note: Roughly 3–5 actions: reproduce the bug, read category.py, identify the two unguarded conditions, apply the two one-line fixes.

interdependence.note: The two edits are largely independent of each other; fixing one does not depend on having fixed the other.

problem:
[Bug]: Confusing deprecation warning when empty data passed to axis with category units
### Bug summary

I'm seeing a `MatplotlibDeprecationWarning` when using calling axes methods on empty data structures for axes that are using string unit converters. I think this is either a false alarm or a non-actionable warning.

### Code for reproduction

```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
f, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.xaxis.update_units(["a", "b"])
ax.plot([], [])
```


### Actual outcome

> MatplotlibDeprecationWarning: Support for passing numbers through unit converters is deprecated since 3.5 and support will be removed two minor releases later; use Axis.convert_units instead.
  ax.plot([], [])

Here's the full traceback if I force the warning to be an error:

<details>

```python-traceback
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
MatplotlibDeprecationWarning              Traceback (most recent call last)
~/miniconda3/envs/seaborn-py39-latest/lib/python3.9/site-packages/matplotlib/axis.py in convert_units(self, x)
   1505         try:
-> 1506             ret = self.converter.convert(x, self.units, self)
   1507         except Exception as e:

~/miniconda3/envs/seaborn-py39-latest/lib/python3.9/site-packages/matplotlib/category.py in convert(value, unit, axis)
     61         if is_numlike:
---> 62             _api.warn_deprecated(
     63                 "3.5", message="Support for passing numbers through unit "

~/miniconda3/envs/seaborn-py39-latest/lib/python3.9/site-packages/matplotlib/_api/deprecation.py in warn_deprecated(since, message, name, alternative, pending, obj_type, addendum, removal)
    100     from . import warn_external
--> 101     warn_external(warning, category=MatplotlibDeprecationWarning)
    102 

~/miniconda3/envs/seaborn-py39-latest/lib/python3.9/site-packages/matplotlib/_api/__init__.py in warn_external(message, category)
    298         frame = frame.f_back
--> 299

difficulty.task_excerpt:
source-read event does not modify include'd files source
### Describe the bug

In [Yocto documentation](https://git.yoc

domain_depth.note: Requires deep familiarity with both Sphinx's extension/event system and docutils internals (StateMachine, directive lifecycle, insert_input), well beyond general Python or web-dev knowledge.

reasoning_complexity.note: The solver must trace how BaseInclude delegates to StateMachine.insert_input(), realize that path bypasses the normal source-read emission, and then design an instance-level monkey-patch that does not recurse.

conceptual_leaps.note: Three non-obvious insights are needed: (1) included files do trigger source-read as documents but their text is injected via a different code path; (2) the fix requires patching the *instance* method to avoid tail-eating recursion; (3) docutils 0.18+ appends two sentinel lines that must be stripped before emitting the event and reattached afterward.

plan_length.note: Roughly 7–9 distinct steps: reproduce the bug, locate Include in other.py, trace BaseInclude → insert_input, design the patch, handle sentinel lines, add imports, wire the conditional check, and verify the output.

interdependence.note: Each step's correctness feeds the next — misunderstanding the sentinel-line convention or choosing a class-level patch instead of an instance-level one invalidates the entire solution.

problem:
source-read event does not modify include'd files source ### Describe the bug In [Yocto documentation](https://git.yoctoproject.org/yocto-docs), we use a custom extension to do some search and replace in literal blocks, see https://git.yoctoproject.org/yocto-docs/tree/documentation/sphinx/yocto-vars.py. We discovered (https://git.yoctoproject.org/yocto-docs/commit/?id=b7375ea4380e716a02c736e4231aaf7c1d868c6b and https://lore.kernel.org/yocto-docs/CAP71WjwG2PCT=ceuZpBmeF-Xzn9yVQi1PG2+d6+wRjouoAZ0Aw@mail.gmail.com/#r) that this does not work on all files and some are left out of this mechanism. Such is the case for include'd files. I could reproduce on Sphinx 5.0.2. ### How to Reproduce conf.py: ```python import sys import os sys.path.insert(0, os.path.abspath('.')) extensions = [ 'my-extension' ] ``` index.rst: ```reStructuredText This is a test ============== .. include:: something-to-include.rst &REPLACE_ME; ``` something-to-include.rst: ```reStructuredText Testing ======= &REPLACE_ME; ``` my-extension.py: ```python #!/usr/bin/env python3 from sphinx.application import Sphinx __version__ = '1.0' def subst_vars_replace(app: Sphinx, docname, source): result = source[0] result = result.replace("&REPLACE_ME;", "REPLACED") source[0] = result def setup(app: Sphinx): app.connect('source-read', subst_vars_replace) return dict( version=__version__, parallel_read_safe=True, parallel_write_safe=True ) ``` ```sh sphinx-build . build if grep -Rq REPLACE_ME build/*.html; then echo BAD; fi ``` `build/index.html` will contain: ```html [...]

Testing¶

&REPLACE_ME;

REPLACED

[...] ``` Note that the dumping docname and source[0] shows that the function actually gets called for something-to-include.

hierarchy.global: Patch Sphinx's included-file reading path so that `source-read` event handler modifications to `source[0]` are applied to content consumed by the `.. include::` directive, not only to top-level document reads.

Determine whether the included file is processed as a full Sphinx document (going through `SphinxFileInput`) or as raw RST bytes spliced inline

Override or patch the `Include` directive's file-reading step to call `_emit_source_read` on the raw bytes before passing them to `insert_input`
